How do you detect a psyop? Let’s take this upcoming situation as a case study.   1. A few weeks ago a woman claiming to be a psychic posts a video crying while saying she received horrifying visions of the June 24th game in Miami where players and fans are abducted by a giant mother ship above the stadium.   2. Today (June 21st) Brazil receives government texts claiming aliens are here. Of course this was purported to be a cyberattack by the Brazilian government.   3. Brazil just happens to rank the highest of any country of alien enthusiast of sightings and lore. I had no idea Brazilians were such dorks, but ok, beam me up Scottie. At least they have good meats and as a Texan, I can respect that.   4. Just so happens that Brazil will be playing in Miami on June 24th — yes the day that the psychic claims to have seen these visions from.   5. Honorable Mention: 2024 New Years Day Miami mall incident where people claimed to see 8 to 10 foot tall humanoids.   It remains to be seen but if something does happen in Miami on June 24th, rest assured, it’s not real aliens, well at least not the extraterrestrial kind. After all this is Miami we’re talking about.

This information came from the Director of the NSA during a Senate Intelligence Committee briefing, but it refers to Mythos' performance during a controlled Red-Team exercise, not a cyberattack on the NSA.  In response, Trump directed Anthropic to cut off access for foreign nationals to Mythos 5 and Fable 5 over national security concerns. Anthropic responded by shutting down access to those models for everyone (not just foreigners) to ensure compliance.

A lot of people are sharing this as: “Anthropic’s Mythos hacked nearly every US classified system in hours.”  That’s misleading.  The original report says Sen. Mark Warner was recounting what the NSA director told him. It does not establish that Mythos independently launched an unauthorized attack on live classified networks, and the test conditions have not been made public.  The real story is still significant but it is not the sci-fi cyberattack many posts are presenting it as.  Original report: economist.com/briefing/2026/…

🚨 NEW: AryStinger — a previously undocumented botnet — has compromised 4,000+ D-Link routers (DIR-850L, DIR-818LW) by exploiting end-of-life vulnerabilities: CVE-2013-3307, CVE-2016-5681, CVE-2025-11837. Infected routers become distributed scanning proxies, tunnels, and command executors for follow-on intrusion operations.  The genius: AryStinger splits massive scanning tasks into parallel chunks across compromised routers, covering their tracks in the process. 48.5% of infections are in South Korea, 31.8% China, with secondary clusters in Sweden, Malaysia, Singapore. A Go-based variant also targets NAS systems. The same router models were previously hit by AVrecon. If you own a D-Link DIR-850L or DIR-818LW, assume it’s compromised. Replace it now. Attackers don’t need every NGINX box.  They need the right config.  CVE-2026-42055 depends on a narrow but real mix: HTTP/2 proxying or gRPC, ignore_invalid_headers off, and header buffers set above 2 MB.  That’s the audit path defenders should not skip.

🛡️ FortiSandbox vulnerable puede convertir tu defensa en puerta de ataque  Fortinet corrigió fallas críticas en FortiSandbox.  FortiSandbox es una herramienta de seguridad que analiza archivos sospechosos para detectar malware antes de que llegue a la red.  El problema es grave porque los atacantes ya están explotando esas fallas contra clientes.  Si una herramienta de defensa se compromete, el atacante gana una posición privilegiada.  Porque podría revisar el entorno, ejecutar comandos o preparar ataques más amplios.  ⚠️ ¿Qué pasó?  Investigadores de VulnCheck observaron explotación activa de CVE-2026-39808 y CVE-2026-39813 en FortiSandbox.  Una CVE es el número público que identifica una vulnerabilidad.  La primera permite inyección de comandos, es decir, mandar instrucciones no autorizadas al sistema.  La segunda es de path traversal, una falla que permite acceder a rutas o archivos que no deberían estar disponibles.  También se observaron intentos contra CVE-2026-25089, otra falla crítica de FortiSandbox.  Los ataques vienen de múltiples países y fuentes, lo que sugiere varios operadores, no una sola campaña.  💡 ¿Qué deben hacer?  Pidan a TIC confirmar si usan FortiSandbox.  Apliquen los parches publicados por Fortinet.  Revisen si FortiSandbox está expuesto a internet.  Monitoreen comandos raros, cambios de configuración y accesos fuera de horario.  Revisen integraciones con otros equipos Fortinet.

Treat matches as potential IoCs and review them against normal admin activity.

One of the more interesting recent Windows privilege escalation vulnerabilities is CVE-2026-42911 in afd.sys (the Ancillary Function Driver for WinSock)  The bug was a use-after-free in the kernel-mode networking subsystem. By triggering the vulnerable code path and reclaiming the freed kernel object with controlled data, an attacker could cause the kernel to dereference a stale pointer and operate on attacker-controlled memory. This memory corruption primitive could be leveraged to gain arbitrary kernel code execution and ultimately elevate privileges from a low-privileged user account to SYSTEM.  What makes the vulnerability notable is that afd.sys has historically been a rich source of kernel exploits, sitting directly on the boundary between user-mode socket APIs and kernel networking internals. Successful exploitation required only local code execution and no user interaction, making it an attractive post-compromise privilege escalation vector. Microsoft patched the flaw in the June 2026 security updates by correcting the underlying object lifetime management in the WinSock driver.

🚨 Someone just gave AI agents the brain of a senior cybersecurity analyst (754 skills, free and open source...)  Here's the details:  AI tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot can write code and search the web. But they don't think like a real security analyst. This project fixes that.  It gives your AI agent a giant playbook of how a senior analyst actually works.  How it works, step by step:  1️⃣ You ask your AI something like: "Check this memory dump for signs of credential theft." 2️⃣ The agent quickly scans all 754 skills (just a few words each) and picks the ones that fit. 3️⃣ It loads the full skill — a clear, step-by-step workflow written by real practitioners. 4️⃣ It runs the exact steps: which tool to use, what to check first, how to execute it. 5️⃣ Then it verifies the result and maps the finding to known attack patterns.  So instead of guessing, your AI follows the same path an expert would. AIエージェントのセキュリティ判断を、人材不足の現場で自走させられるか——その材料が6月20日に動いた。  ai.whytrend.jp/x/e1f7d217f58…  【数値】GitHub Trendingで1日+336★、総16,885★へ急上昇  有志が公開したセキュリティ技能集が1日で336件のスターを集め、GitHubトレンド入りした。中身は「上級分析者がどの場面でどの手法を使い、何を前提確認し、結果をどう検証するか」という判断手順を754個に構造化したもの。攻撃コードのリポジトリではなく、分析者の思考手順そのものをエージェントに渡せる点が従来と違う。  ■ 754個の技能が動かす現場コスト  ・26分野754個の技能を1リポジトリに集約。概要走査を約30トークンに抑えた設計で、エージェントの文脈枠（処理できる情報量の上限）を圧迫せず全技能を一度に探索できる。 ・Claude Code・Cursor・GitHub Copilotなど26以上の開発支援ツールでそのまま使える。既存の環境に追加するだけで、定型のセキュリティ分析をエージェントに委ねる試行が始められる。  ■ コンプライアンス対応との接続——得する組織と確認が要る組織  ・全754技能をMITRE ATT&CK（攻撃手法の分類体系）、NIST CSF 2.0（サイバーセキュリティの国際標準枠組み）、NIST AI RMF（AIリスク管理の枠組み）など5基準に対応付け済み。社内のコンプライアンス文書と照合すれば、技能と監査根拠を同じ基準で紐づけられる。 ・ただし本プロジェクトはAnthropic公式ではなく独立した有志による制作。Apache 2.0ライセンスで商用利用は可能だが、継続的なサポートや保証は自前で評価する必要がある。公式調達を前提とする組織は、導入前にメンテナンス体制の確認を先に行う。  ■ 誰の判断コストが動くか  ・セキュリティ運用チーム: 上級分析者の判断手順を後付けでエージェントに組み込めるため、人材不足の中で定型分析の自走化を試せる。社内報告の根拠も5基準への対応表でそのまま示せる。 ・Claude Code・Cursor利用中の開発チーム: 既に使っているツールに1分野だけ読み込ませる小さな試行から始められる。導入コストの入口が低い。 ・公式サポートを調達条件とする組織: 有志制作という性質上、ベンダー保証を前提とした調達フローには乗らない。評価コストを別途見積もる必要がある。  ■ 次の一手——48時間以内に確認・照合・試す3点  ・確認する: リポジトリのApache 2.0ライセンス条文と、直近の更新頻度・コントリビューター体制を確認し、自社の継続利用に耐えるか判断する。 ・照合する: 自社が準拠するNIST CSF 2.0やNIST AI RMFの対象範囲と、754技能の対応表が社内コンプライアンス文書とどこまで重なるかを照合する。 ・試す: 既に使うClaude CodeやCursorに1分野の技能だけ読み込ませ、概要走査30トークンで文脈枠が圧迫されないかを実測する。  詳細な技能構造・5基準の対応表・導入手順は記事本文で。👇

自分用に調べたので、日本のCSIRTメンバーに共有  - mukul975 氏作のサイバーセキュリティAIスキル集。754個のSOC/DFIR/脅威インテリ実務手順書(SKILL.md)＋1,041本の参照用Pythonヘルパーで構成 - MITRE ATT&CK / NIST CSF など主要5フレームワークにマッピング、Claude Code等で利用可能  【公式性・信頼性】 - Anthropic公式ではない（名前にAnthropicが入るが README に「Not affiliated with Anthropic PBC」と明記。詐称ではなくコミュニティプロジェクトと正直に表示） - スター17.1k / Fork 2.1k / Apache-2.0 / 直近も活発に更新。作者は2018年からのアカウントでセキュリティ専攻、一定の信頼性あり - 軽セキュリティ監査では悪性コード（任意実行・難読化・認証情報窃取・外部送信）は未検出。スクリプトは自動実行ではなく参照素材  【既存スキルとの重複・差分】 - ほぼ重複なし。既存の security-audit / vuln-scan / threat-model 等は「自分のコードを監査する」プロセス系 - 本リポジトリはマルウェア解析・脅威ハンティング等の実務ドメイン知識系で、用途は補完的  【導入是非の結論】条件付き推奨（フルコピーは非推奨、選択導入を推奨） - 理由1: 754スキル全部の導入はコンテキスト肥大・トークン浪費。通常業務とは大半が無関係 - 理由2: ただしSOC/DFIR実務が発生した時の参照知識としては価値が高く品質も良い - 推奨運用: git clone で参照用に置き、必要な数スキルだけ手元の skills/ に取り込む。導入前に対象スクリプトを必ず目視確認（非公式のため自己責任）

Google DeepMind just published their AI Control Roadmap. The framing should disturb you: they treat internal AI agents as potential insider threats. The same way a company treats a rogue employee who already has office access.  The roadmap uses the MITRE ATT&CK framework, originally built for tracking human adversary tactics, to model what an AI agent might do if it goes off track. Trusted AI supervisors constantly review the working agent's reasoning, actions, and plans. If the supervisor sees something harmful, it blocks the action before damage occurs.  For low-risk actions, they review transcripts after the fact. For high-risk actions like major cyber attacks, they block in real time. Detection levels D1 through D4. Prevention levels R1 through R3.  Meanwhile, a new paper called Aegis proposes cryptographic runtime governance for autonomous AI. Each agent gets bound to an Immutable Ethics Policy Layer at system genesis. Policy-violating actions are not discouraged or flagged. They are rendered operationally non-executable. Verified violations trigger autonomous shutdown and generate auditable proof artifacts.  The binary signal: the industry has moved from how do we align AI to how do we contain AI that we know is imperfectly aligned. The assumption is no longer that alignment works. The assumption is that alignment will fail and we need defense in depth when it does.  The question is not whether AI will go off track. The question is whether you catch it before or after the damage.

Stuxnet was first publicly identified in 2010, but it had already been running inside Iran's Natanz enrichment facility for roughly two to three years before anyone outside the operation knew it existed. That gap matters. The weapon worked in silence, and the silence was part of the design.  The technical architecture is worth understanding in full, because the doctrine followed from it. Stuxnet targeted Siemens programmable logic controllers governing Iranian centrifuges, issuing commands that caused the centrifuges to physically destroy themselves while simultaneously feeding operators false readings showing normal performance. The machines were tearing themselves apart while the instruments said everything was fine. That combination, physical destruction plus sensor spoofing, was not an accident of engineering. It was a strategic choice about how to maximize damage while minimizing the window for response. Four separate zero-day vulnerabilities. Self-replicating propagation. Precision targeting logic designed to contain collateral spread. Industrial control system-specific payload. No one had built anything like it before, at least not anything that became publicly known.  The United States and Israel developed it jointly. That has never been formally acknowledged by either government, but it has also never been seriously disputed. The New York Times reporting in 2012, drawing on sources with direct knowledge, established the outline. NPR's June 2026 analysis treats it as established baseline, which it is.  What the NPR piece is doing, sixteen years after public discovery, is asking what Stuxnet actually built. The answer is uncomfortable.  The proof of concept Stuxnet demonstrated was this: a nation-state could cause physical destruction to an adversary's strategic infrastructure using only software, with plausible deniability, without a single soldier crossing a border, without a missile or a bomb, and with effects precise enough to avoid the kind of obvious provocation that triggers a conventional military response. That combination had never existed before. Once it was demonstrated, the logic was available to everyone.  Iran's response is the clearest direct line. After Natanz, Iran invested heavily in offensive cyber capabilities. This is documented. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Cyber Command grew substantially. Iranian APT groups, variously designated by researchers as APT33, APT34, APT35, and others, developed capabilities across espionage, disruption, and destructive attack. The Shamoon malware that hit Saudi Aramco in 2012, destroying data on roughly 30,000 workstations, came roughly two years after Stuxnet's public exposure. Iranian attribution to Shamoon has been widely assessed, though Tehran denied it. The timing and the context are not coincidental.  Russia had its own trajectory, but the Stuxnet precedent validated a doctrine Russian military planners had been developing conceptually. The attacks on Ukrainian power infrastructure in December 2015 and December 2016, using BlackEnergy and Industroyer respectively, were the operational expression of a Stuxnet-derived logic applied to a different target set. Lights went out in parts of Ukraine. The attackers had persistent access to industrial control systems. The operations were calibrated to cause disruption without triggering a full military response from NATO. That calibration, that space between demonstrable harm and the threshold of conventional war, is exactly the space Stuxnet proved existed.  China's approach has been somewhat different in emphasis. Chinese APT groups, including the cluster now tracked as Volt Typhoon, have been documented engaging in what U.S. intelligence has described as pre-positioning: achieving persistent access to American critical infrastructure including water systems, power grids, and communications networks, not for immediate exploitation but for potential wartime activation or coercive leverage. The CISA and NSA advisory on Volt Typhoon issued in 2024 was unusually direct about the assessed intent. This is not espionage infrastructure. It is sabotage infrastructure held in reserve. The concept of holding that kind of access, positioning it quietly for later use, descends directly from the Stuxnet model.  North Korea's cyber program is more financially motivated in its primary focus, but it has demonstrated destructive capability when directed. The Sony Pictures attack in 2014. WannaCry in 2017, which the U.S. UK, and others formally attributed to the Lazarus Group in 2018 and which caused somewhere between four and eight billion dollars in global damage. The destructive capacity is real and it has been used.  The proliferation dynamic NPR is documenting in 2026 is this: a capability that took extraordinary resources to develop in 2007 is now within reach of any state with a serious cyber investment and a few years of sustained effort. The technical barrier has dropped. The doctrine has spread. And the infrastructure for what the analysis calls "pre-positioned deterrence" now exists across multiple competing powers simultaneously.  The original rationale for Stuxnet, as best as can be reconstructed from reporting, was to buy time. The Natanz centrifuges were enriching uranium. The alternative being weighed was an Israeli airstrike, with all the escalatory risk that carried. A covert operation that set back the program by months or years without triggering a war looked like the better option. NPR frames it as a weapon that may have prevented a conventional military strike. That framing is defensible.  The cost of that choice is what the June 2026 analysis is actually about. The same logic that made Stuxnet attractive, deniable, precise, below the threshold of open war, made it a template that adversaries would inevitably adopt and adapt. Every state actor with serious capabilities now holds some version of this in reserve. The targets include power grids, water treatment, financial systems, communications infrastructure. The access has in many documented cases already been achieved. What has not happened yet, in most cases, is the decision to activate it.  That is the world Stuxnet built. Not a world where one country's centrifuges spun out of control in 2008. A world where multiple major powers have pre-positioned destructive access inside each other's critical systems and are making continuous calculations about whether the conditions for use have arrived.  The deterrence logic here is genuinely unstable in ways that nuclear deterrence, for all its horrors, is not. Nuclear weapons require physical delivery. The infrastructure for cyber pre-positioning is already inside the target. The action required to activate it is a software command. The attribution window can be narrow or disputed. The escalation ladder is not well-defined. There is no established treaty framework, no inspections regime, no red phone dedicated to this category of conflict. There have been bilateral cyber discussions between the U.S. and China, and there are norms conversations happening in various multilateral forums, but nothing binding, nothing with teeth.  Sixteen years from public discovery, Stuxnet's most durable legacy is not the centrifuges it destroyed. Those were eventually rebuilt. The legacy is the doctrine it proved, and the infrastructure that doctrine has since generated across every major state actor in the system.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #MalwareWarfare #IndustrialSabotage #ZeroDayExploitation #CriticalInfrastructureMapping #InfrastructureAttacks #CyberCapabilityDevelopment

In 2007, North Korea stood up a hacking unit that would eventually steal more than a billion dollars in a single year. The organizational decision to do it was made in 2003, after watching the U.S. invasion of Iraq. Pyongyang drew the obvious lesson: conventional military competition with the United States was a dead end. Cyber was the asymmetric play.  The unit that became Lazarus Group was built as a subordinate element under Lab 110, itself nested within Bureau 121 of the Reconnaissance General Bureau. The RGB is North Korea's primary foreign intelligence service, and Bureau 121 has long been identified as its main cyber arm. Recent intelligence analysis suggests Lab 110 is probably a reorganized, expanded version of Bureau 121 rather than a separate entity sitting above it. The hierarchy beneath Lab 110 includes TEMP.Hermit, APT38, and Andariel as distinct subordinate units with their own operational mandates. This isn't one team of hackers. It's a structured organization with divisions.  The groundwork went back further than 2007. North Korea started sending computer scientists abroad in the 1990s to acquire technical knowledge, then brought them home. By the early 2000s, the regime had made cyberwarfare a top institutional priority. The 2007 formalization of Lazarus Group was the organizational crystallization of that investment.  Initial activity focused on malware development and reconnaissance. The first significant public demonstration came in July 2009 with Operation Troy, which hit at least 39 websites using a botnet of somewhere between 20,000 and 166,000 hijacked machines. The range on that number reflects how hard attribution was even for relatively blunt operations. That difficulty was part of the point. North Korea had correctly assessed that the international framework for attributing and responding to cyberattacks was still being invented, and that an early mover with deniable capabilities could cause serious damage with limited consequence.  Operation Troy established the operational continuity that ran through DarkSeoul in 2013, the Sony Pictures hack in 2014, and the WannaCry ransomware campaign in 2017. Each operation was different in target and method. What they shared was organizational persistence. The same institutional structure kept producing attacks across a decade and a half.  By 2013, high-profile defectors were telling intelligence communities that North Korea had approximately 6,000 trained hackers. That figure, if accurate, represents a substantial national commitment to a single capability, particularly for a country of North Korea's size and economic constraints. Current assessments place the DPRK's cyber sophistication in the same general tier as China and Russia. That's a significant thing to say about a country that in most other military-technical domains is frozen somewhere around the 1970s.  The financial dimension is where the modern operation becomes hard to look away from. DPRK cyber actors stole at least $1.19 billion in 2024. One year. The proceeds move through cryptocurrency and get used to procure military equipment, funding a sanctions-constrained weapons program through digital theft. The regime has built a revenue stream that doesn't require agricultural exports, diplomatic concessions, or Chinese goodwill. It requires servers and skilled people.  The IT worker scheme runs alongside the straight theft. North Korean nationals use stolen identities to get hired at American companies, over 100 of them by recent Justice Department count. The Justice Department has seized 29 laptop farms across 16 states. These aren't sophisticated covers. In many cases, someone in the U.S. is physically operating the laptop and maintaining the fiction of a domestic employee. The wages go back to Pyongyang. The access goes to intelligence collection or future intrusion.  The Lazarus Group formation in 2007 is worth marking precisely because it was so deliberate. This wasn't a capability that emerged from freelancers or opportunistic criminal enterprise that the state later co-opted. It was a planned institutional response to a strategic problem, built from the ground up inside an intelligence service, staffed by people the state had spent years training abroad. The decision to build it preceded the formal stand-up by four years. The returns on that decision have compounded for nearly two decades.  Other states watched and drew conclusions. The model, offensive cyber as a low-attribution, high-return asymmetric tool operated through a dedicated unit inside military intelligence, is not unique to North Korea anymore. But North Korea ran the proof of concept. The 2007 formation is where that particular experiment got serious.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CyberCapabilityDevelopment #MalwareDistribution

I also once messed up an rsync command and synced my tax documents to the staff at DEFCON. They expected malware, instead they got my birth certificate, my tax returns, and some other various documents.  Do I panic? No, I deal with malware every single day. I collect malware, reverse engineer malware, write malware, etc. It is more annoying than actually concerning. The only time I was genuinely concerned was when I detonated REvil because I usually don't make backups (I'm not a coward).  Basically, I'm a jackass who has gotten way too comfortable with malware and you should not do what I do.

sample_tweets.text: Defi를 믿을 수 없다면, 이제는 @CapApp  이 나서야 할 때 ( @Benjamin918_  and @fireant_korea 인터뷰 영상 후기)  제 크립토 경력은 길지 않습니다. 이제 막 2년차를 맞이하고 있으나, DeFi가 지금 얼마나 위험한 상황에 처했는지는 알고 있습니다.  개인의 실수로 인해 해킹당하는 것 이외에도, 프로토콜이 해킹을 당한다거나, 혹은 러그풀로 인해 돈이 사라지는 등 그야말로 탈중앙화를 빌미로 한 무법천지의 세상이 되어버렸죠.  더욱더 비극인 점은 CAP의 파운더인 Benjamin의 말처럼 프로토콜 스스로의 도덕적 해이입니다.   Fantom의 대출 프로토콜 중 한 프로젝트(Scream)에서 핵심 관계자가 러그풀을 하고 싶다는 등 입에 담기 힘든 말을 하기도 했으며, 실제로 Scream이라는 프로토콜은 fUSD와 DEI라는 스테이블코인의 디페깅을 악용한 차익거래로 풀이 고갈되어 말 그대로 무너져버리고 말았습니다.  오라클과 한도 설정을 잘못한 리스크 관리의 실패라는, DeFi의 뻔하디뻔한 결말 중 하나지만 여기에는 프로토콜과 그 관계자들조차 믿어선 안 된다는 교훈을 담고 있습니다.  현재 CAP의 CTO인 Weso도 당시의 위험을 옆에서 겪었고, 간신히 자금을 빼내는 데에 성공해 피해를 줄였다고 하죠. 그리고 그 교훈이 Cap으로 이어지게 되었습니다.  Cap에서 발행하는 cUSD라는 스테이블코인은, 디페깅을 구조에서 막아두었습니다. 0.05%라는 타이트한 가격 오차 범위를 설정해둔 것은 물론이고, 상환 구조에서도 방지해두었습니다. cUSD를 되찾을 때 담보 바스켓 전체를 비율대로 그대로 돌려주어, 늦게 빠져나온 사람이 디페깅된 자산을 다 떠안는 문제도 방지했죠.  여기에 그치지 않고 Benjamin이 말하듯 CAP은 '편집증적'이라 할 수 있는 보안 시스템을 추가했습니다. Beefy 출신 엔지니어들이 대거 합류했기에, 시스템 붕괴의 두려움을 누구보다 잘 알고 있기 때문일 겁니다.  단순히 핵심 관계자들이 보안에 신경 써야 한다는 수준의 뻔하디 뻔한 격언 같은 게 아니라, AI 코드 감사 도구인 Octane으로 배포 전 코드를 일일이 검증하고, 실시간 모니터링 시스템인 Hypernative를 도입해 멤풀을 감시합니다.  해킹 징후가 잡히는 즉시, 사람 손을 거치지 않아도 프로토콜 전체가 즉시 멈추게 되어있는데, 그것도 24시간 365일 내내 감시가 됩니다. 여기에 Certora 정식 감사와 멀티시그, 추가 서명 절차 같은 기본도 당연히 갖췄습니다.  CAP의 특징은 수익원도 크립토에 한정하지 않겠다는 겁니다. 현재 자본 시장의 자금은 AI와 반도체로 흐르고 있습니다. 크립토 안에서 한정된 자원을 두고 수익을 만드는 건 한계에 봉착했죠.   그래서 CAP은 실물 경제로 눈을 돌려 로보틱스와 농업, 제조 분야의 실물 대출 시장과 함께 중동의 RWA 시장으로까지 확대하려는 로드맵을 그리고 있습니다.   토큰화된 금과 디르함 기반 대출 상품이 준비된다면 DeFi가 온체인에서 머무르지 않고 오프체인까지 아우르는 금융 자산이 될 수도 있을 듯합니다.  DeFi를 복잡하고 위험해서 멀리했던 사람들에게 어떻게 접근해야 하는지, CAP은 한국 시장의 이용자들에 대한 관심을 통해 방법을 제시하는 것 같습니다. 스테이블코인조차도 리스크는 0이 아니지만, Cap은 이를 좀 더 '접근 가능한 금융상품'으로 제공하기 위해 노력하는 것처럼 보입니다.

BYD OTA 업데이트 공지. (6월 19일 공지)  ATTO 3, 씰, 돌핀 전 라인업에 6월 19일 자로 대규모 패치가 떴는데요.  스마트폰이 아니라 시속 100km로 달리는 '움직이는 기기'가 무선으로 업데이트라......  이번 업데이트, 단순 UI 개선이 아닙니다. 지능형 크루즈 컨트롤(ICC),  코너 주행 시 감속 로직까지— 주행 제어 영역을 손댔습니다.  테슬라가 연 '소프트웨어 중심 자동차(SDV)' 시대, 제조사에겐 비용 절감이지만 소비자에겐 보안 리스크의 시작입니다.  중국산 전기차 백도어 우려, 단순 반중 정서일까요?  작년 글로벌 자동차 보안 기업들의 화이트 해킹 실험 결과는 충격적이었습니다. 차량 제어 내부망(CAN 버스)에 침투하는 순간 브레이크를 풀고 조향 장치를 꺾는 것까지  원격으로 가능했습니다.  BYD도 'UN R155' 같은 국제 사이버보안 규제를 맞추며 한국 진출을 준비 중이지만, 무선 연결(OTA) 통로가 열려있다는 것 자체가 해커에겐 가장 매력적인 타겟입니다.  편의성을 얻은 대가로 우리는 '달리는 해킹 위험'을  떠안게 된 셈입니다.  기능이 화려해질수록 차를 살 때 '보안 등급'부터 따져야 하는 날이 머지않았습니다.  무선 업데이트되는 차, 100% 신뢰하고 타실 수 있나요?  📌 나중에 차 고를 때 참고하도록  북마크 해두세요.

미국이 직접 조치를 시작할때는 단순한 한미공조 수사가 아니라 미국을 주축으로 부정선거에 피해를 입은 국가들이 연합한 국제 합동 부정선거 수사단을 출범시켜 국제적으로 압박해 들어올 가능성이 높습니다.   아래는 가상 시나리오로 AI에 물어본 내용입니다. ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡ  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

sample_tweets.text: It does seem like chaos. I've come to the conclusion that it's supposed to!  There's a coalition that has formed. UAE has dropped out of OPEC and CLOSELY allied with Israel!  Who would have thought that could happen a year ago?  I'm as eager as anyone to see the regime exterminated. But, I also know that if there's to be a lasting peace, Iran's neighbors are going to have to be the ones to keep it. We can't occupy the region for the next generation.  I believe it can be done.  As for an attack on our soil...  Count on it!  There are 18,000 persons here with KNOWN ties to Isis and Tehran. They came in under the last administration.  There are also regime loyalists coming in from the Canadian border.  There are a variety of theories with just a few known facts.  Among these facts are that there are about 4,000 who came in to instruct, train, and coordinate the rest. There has been a homeland plot for several years. That plot has been postponed and delayed only because key individuals who were responsible for giving the official "go" have been eliminated.  The intended participants (terrorists) are modifying their strategies and rethinking the "go" signals.  High population events; churches, synagogues, trauma centers... these are all targets for kinetic and cyber attacks. Some, simultaneously.  So.  Count on there being an attack.  The "homeland" plot was originally intended to be larger than 9/11.  These plans are directed at major cities for maximum media coverage. The possibility of it happening here, in New Hampshire, is extremely remote. But, the possibility does exist.  The best advise is to do what I'd advise anyone to do in their normal day-to-day lives whether they live in a city or out in the pucker-brush.  Don't panic!  Keep your eyes open. Keep your attention on your surroundings. Always know who is in your immediate proximity.  And, as one gentleman (@christianfiveoh)  puts it;   Remember your "A. B. C.s" Always Be Carrying.  God Bless. Remember: It's God's Plan, not ours. We'll get through this.  PS...  Sorry this is so long-winded.

Today on YouTube: President Trump gives Iran an Ultimatum. (i.e. He told them if they didn't "straighten up" he would take over control of Iran and the Strait of Hormuz).  GROK what is the likelihood President Trump will follow through on his threat by actually ordering the US Military to take control of Iran and the Strait?  What obstacles would preclude this action by the United States?  What could Iran actually do to stop this from happening?  Use right leaning sources.  GROK says:  Very low likelihood overall for a full US military takeover of Iran itself; moderate to high for enforcing control or influence over the Strait of Hormuz through naval means if diplomacy collapses. foxnews.com  The YouTube claim appears to exaggerate or sensationalize recent events. As of June 2026, President Trump has issued multiple ultimatums and threats focused on forcing Iran to keep the Strait of Hormuz fully open and toll-free for shipping (critical for ~20% of global oil trade). He has threatened strikes on Iranian infrastructure (power plants, bridges, oil facilities like Kharg Island), imposed a US naval blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait area, and more recently warned of US-imposed tolls or direct control of the chokepoint as a “guardian angel” if no final deal is reached within timelines like 60 days. euronews.com  There are no credible reports or statements from Trump or his administration indicating plans to invade and occupy the entire country of Iran (population ~90 million, vast difficult terrain). Threats have centered on targeted strikes, economic pressure, and naval enforcement of the Strait—not regime change via ground invasion or full territorial control. Earlier conflict phases (US/Israel strikes on Iran starting ~early 2026, following tensions and leader losses like Supreme Leader Khamenei) involved airstrikes and responses, not a broad US ground campaign. Negotiations for a framework/MOU (reopen Strait toll-free, lift blockade elements, nuclear limits, etc.) have been ongoing amid fragile ceasefires, with recent stalling and renewed threats. foxnews.com  Likelihood Assessment (Right-Leaning Perspective)  Right-leaning sources like Fox News portray Trump’s approach as strong, America First leverage: using US naval superiority to counter Iranian “extortion” via the Strait, issuing escalating deadlines while extending them for talks, and preferring deals that secure US interests (free passage, no Iranian tolls) over endless commitments. foxnews.com  Full takeover/control of Iran (invasion/occupation):   Near zero.  Trump’s base and “America First” doctrine strongly oppose new quagmires like Iraq/Afghanistan. Analyses aligned with conservative views note no political appetite for the massive ground forces, sustained occupation, or insurgency risks required. Trump has repeatedly paused threats, extended deadlines, and pursued negotiations/deals even amid escalation. arabcenterdc.org  Control/enforcement over the Strait of Hormuz:   Higher (already partially enacted via blockade;      could expand to toll collection or sustained naval dominance if deals fail). Trump has explicitly discussed US control or tolls as leverage, and Fox reporting highlights successful US naval operations protecting shipping. foxnews.com  Trump has a pattern of maximum pressure + deal-making (e.g., past Soleimani strike, Abraham Accords, recent MOU efforts). Full invasion contradicts this and his record of avoiding prolonged wars.  Key Obstacles Precluding Full Action on Iran  Even if Trump wanted to escalate dramatically, major barriers exist (consistent with right-leaning skepticism of nation-building):  Political/Domestic:       Strong opposition from parts of his own party and base to new Middle East wars. Public support exists for targeted pressure but not occupation. Midterm elections (Nov 2026) would amplify risks. Some conservatives criticize deals as too soft but reject quagmires. reuters.com  Military/Logistical:       Iran’s size, terrain (mountains, deserts), IRGC capabilities, missiles/drones, and potential insurgency make sustained occupation extremely costly in lives and resources. US forces are stretched; lessons      from past interventions loom large. Targeted strikes or naval ops are feasible; full invasion is not. arabcenterdc.org  Economic:       Major disruption to global oil flows would spike prices, risk recession, and hurt US allies (even if US is more energy-independent). Right-leaning views emphasize protecting US economic interests without self-harm.  Legal/International:       War Powers Resolution requires congressional involvement for sustained operations. Backlash from China, Russia, and others; limited allied ground      support.  Strategic:       Diverts focus from China/Russia priorities. Right-leaning analysts favor pressure, sanctions, and deals (Venezuela-style oil leverage) over occupation. rand.org  What Iran Could Do to Deter or Stop Escalation  Iran has asymmetric tools to raise costs and push back toward negotiation (as seen in past responses and current stalling):  Close or Disrupt the Strait:  Mine the waterway, attack tankers/shipping (as      historically done), or restrict passage to create global economic pressure and international calls for de-escalation.  Asymmetric/Proxies:       Activate or support groups (Houthis, Iraqi militias, remnants of Hezbollah influence) to strike US interests, bases, or shipping lanes, increasing US      casualties and domestic political costs.  Direct Military:  Missile/drone barrages on US forces, Israel, or regional      targets; cyber attacks on US critical infrastructure.  Nuclear Brinkmanship:  Accelerate enrichment or threaten breakout to force talks or deter strikes.  Diplomacy/Delay:       Stall negotiations, walk out of talks (as recently reported), rally domestic support by framing resistance, or seek mediation from China/Russia to split US resolve.  Internal Resilience:  Regime has shown ability to absorb strikes and maintain control, using propaganda to unify against external threats.  These raise the price of US action without requiring conventional victory, potentially forcing Trump back to deal-making (his preferred path per patterns in Fox reporting). foxnews.com  In summary, expect continued tough rhetoric, naval presence, and pressure for a deal securing free Strait passage and limits on Iran. How to Protect Yourself and Respond 1.  Secure your devices: •  Change all passwords (use a password manager) and enable 2FA (two-factor authentication). Avoid sharing codes. •  Check for unknown apps/devices: On iPhone, review Settings > [Your Name] > Find My or Family Sharing. On Android, check Google account activity and location sharing. •  Scan for malware/stalkerware with reputable tools (e.g., Malwarebytes, or factory reset as a last resort after backing up data). •  Turn off location services for non-essential apps; review app permissions. Disable unnecessary family sharing. avg. com  2.  Limit exposure: •  Make social media accounts private, review followers, and avoid check-ins or oversharing. •  Use a VPN for added privacy on public Wi-Fi. •  Create new accounts if needed, and avoid linking to old ones. 3.  Document and report: •  Keep records of incidents, messages, and access attempts. •  Report to platforms (social media has harassment/stalking tools). •  Contact police if you feel unsafe—provide evidence. In many areas, you can seek a restraining order or protection order covering digital contact. •  Reach out to hotlines: In the U.S., National Domestic Violence Hotline (thehotline. org or 1-800-799-7233) or Stalking Resource Center. Similar services exist internationally (e.g., eSafety in Australia). 4.  If it’s parents monitoring you as a teen/minor: •  Open conversation about trust and boundaries may help. For extreme cases, talk to a trusted adult, counselor, or child protective services if it feels abusive. 5.  Seek support: •  Therapy can help process the violation of trust (family betrayal hits hard). •  Safety planning resources from domestic abuse organizations cover tech abuse specifically. stopstalkerware. org   You’re not overreacting—privacy matters, and no family tie entitles someone to spy on you. It involves repeated unwanted monitoring or intrusion that causes fear or distress. fitzpatrick. house. gov   Signs This Might Be Happening Common indicators include: •  Neighbors knowing details about your private conversations, online activity, location, or plans that you haven’t shared publicly. •  Unusual device behavior: rapid battery drain, overheating, high data usage, unexpected restarts, or strange noises during calls. surfshark. com  •  Unknown apps, accounts logged in on your devices, or suspicious changes in settings. •  They reference things from your social media or emails in person. •  Suspicious Wi-Fi activity, like unauthorized access to your network. Note: Some “signs” can have innocent explanations (e.g., coincidence or shared networks), so document patterns objectively.  Immediate Steps to Protect Yourself 1.  Secure your accounts and devices: •  Change all passwords to strong, unique ones (use a password manager). •  Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA/2FA) everywhere, preferably app-based rather than SMS. •  Check for unknown devices logged into your accounts (e.g., Google, Apple, social media) and remove them. •  Update your OS, apps, and router firmware. Use antivirus/malware scanners (reputable ones like Malwarebytes). •  Review app permissions, especially camera/microphone/location access. Remove suspicious apps. •  For Wi-Fi: Change your router password, use WPA3 encryption, and hide your SSID if possible. Consider a VPN for added privacy. consumer.ftc. gov  2.  Limit what you share: •  Tighten social media privacy settings (make accounts private, limit who sees posts/stories). •  Avoid posting real-time location info. •  Be cautious with smart home devices (cameras, assistants) that could be compromised. thehotline. org  3.  Document everything: •  Keep a log of incidents: dates, times, what happened, screenshots, messages, etc. •  Save evidence without engaging the neighbor—this strengthens your case. instagram. com   Report and Seek Help •  Contact local police: File a report for stalking or harassment. Provide your documentation. Cyberstalking is a crime in many jurisdictions (e.g., under U.S. federal and state laws). Mention any physical surveillance too (cameras pointed at your property can also be illegal depending on intent and local rules). fitzpatrick. house. gov  •  If devices are compromised: Report to the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3. gov) for hacking-related issues. ic3. gov  •  Get professional help: •  National Domestic Violence Hotline (US): 1-800-799-7233 or thehotline. org (they handle tech abuse/stalking). •  Local victim advocates or legal aid for restraining orders. •  A digital security expert or “bug sweep” service if you suspect physical devices (AirTags, hidden cameras). gabbypetitofoundation. org  Do not confront the neighbors directly or try vigilante tech countermeasures—this can escalate risks or complicate legal action. If you feel unsafe immediately, call 911.  Prevention for the Future •  Use strong network security and monitor connected devices. •  Be mindful of what you discuss near windows or shared spaces. •  Consider cameras on your own property (legally) for your protection.  If this is ongoing and causing significant distress, consult a lawyer specializing in privacy or harassment. Laws vary by location, so local resources are best. We earnestly request the team to accelerate the implementation of various ecosystem initiatives while ensuring security and technological stability, seizing the window of opportunity for industry growth.  Fifth, we suggest opening a KYC error correction channel. For users experiencing system instability, operational errors, or incorrect data uploads, we earnestly request the official team to provide a unified opportunity for resubmission and error correction, balancing humanization and fairness, and retaining every loyal community user.  We sincerely hope that under the leadership of KV and the ITL Foundation, InterLink will steadily accelerate its development, implementing step by step, towards a higher and farther-reaching ecosystem future.  @kv_interlink @reina_itl @leduy4792 @Scott_itlg #InterLink #ITLG #ITL #ITLX

gemini :  제시된 소스코드의 핵심 취약점은 버퍼 오버플로우(Buffer Overflow), 그중에서도 스택 버퍼 오버플로우(Stack Buffer Overflow)입니다.  구체적인 원인과 공격 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 취약점의 핵심 원인: gets() 함수 사용  read_username 함수 내부를 보면 사용자 입력을 받기 위해 gets() 함수를 사용하고 있습니다.  C  void read_username(User *user) {     gets(user->username); // <--- 심각한 취약점 발생 지점 } gets()의 위험성: gets() 함수는 입력받을 버퍼의 크기를 검사하지 않고, 줄바꿈 문자(\n)나 EOF를 만날 때까지 무조건 입력을 받아 메모리에 저장합니다. 버퍼의 크기: User 구조체에서 username은 16바이트(char username[16];)로 선언되어 있습니다. 만약 사용자가 16바이트를 초과하는 문자열을 입력하면, 제한된 메모리 공간을 넘어서서 뒤에 있는 메모리 영역을 침범(덮어쓰기)하게 됩니다. 2. 취약점 악용 시나리오 (데이터 변조)  메모리 상에서 User 구조체의 멤버 변수들은 대략 다음과 같이 연속적으로 배치됩니다.  메모리 영역username (16 바이트)isAdmin (4 바이트)초기 값"" (비어있음)0 (일반 사용자) 만약 공격자가 username 입력 창에 16바이트를 꽉 채우고 추가로 데이터를 더 입력한다면, 바로 옆에 붙어 있는 isAdmin 변수의 메모리 공간을 덮어쓰게 됩니다.  정상 입력 (15글자 이하): isAdmin 변수가 0으로 유지되어 "Normal User"가 출력됩니다.  공격 입력 (예: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA): 앞의 A 16개는 username에 들어가고, 넘쳐흐른 뒤쪽의 A 데이터가 isAdmin 자리를 0이 아닌 값(예: 0x41414141)으로 오염시킵니다.  C언어에서는 0이 아닌 모든 값을 참(True)으로 취급하므로, if (user.isAdmin) 조건문이 참이 되어 "Admin Access Granted"(관리자 권한 획득)가 실행됩니다. 3. 해결 방법 (보안 코딩) 입력받을 버퍼의 크기를 제한할 수 있는 안전한 함수인 fgets()를 사용해야 합니다. C void read_username(User *user) {     // sizeof(user->username)을 통해 최대 16바이트까지만 안전하게 입력받음     fgets(user->username, sizeof(user->username), stdin); }

Me: Common dark web plots that targets individuals  Ai: Common “plots” or schemes on the dark web that target or affect individuals typically involve stolen personal data being sold or used for extortion, fraud, and scams. Criminals harvest data through breaches, malware, or phishing, then monetize it on underground markets. Regular people (not just dark web users) are the primary victims, often via surface-web attacks like emails or fake sites. aura. com   These are not recommendations—engaging with the dark web for illegal purposes is risky and often illegal. Most “services” there are scams themselves. The focus here is awareness to help protect against threats. 1. Sale of Stolen Personal Data (Data Dumps and Credential Markets) •  Hackers sell batches of stolen info: emails, passwords, SSNs, credit card details, bank logins, medical records, addresses, and more. experian. com  •  How it targets individuals: Buyers use this for identity theft, account takeovers, or crafting personalized phishing. Old breach data is recycled, mixed with fakes, or sold as “fresh.” •  Prices are low (e.g., credit cards for $25 or hacked accounts for $20–$60). This fuels downstream fraud like unauthorized purchases or loan applications. crowdstrike. com  •  Impact: Victims notice strange charges, denied loans, or tax fraud in their name. 2. Sextortion and Blackmail Scams •  One of the fastest-growing threats. Scammers claim to have compromising videos/photos (often faked or from malware on adult sites) and threaten to send them to contacts/family unless paid in crypto. troopers.ny. gov  •  Data from dark web leaks (emails, personal details, photos of homes) makes threats seem credible. Automated bulk emails or targeted messages are common. •  Variants: Fake “hacked webcam” claims, romance-based coercion (especially targeting teens/young men), or deepfake extortion. pandasecurity. com  •  Impact: Thousands of reports yearly; victims feel shame and pay, but demands often continue. 3. Phishing, Malware Distribution, and Ransomware Kits •  Dark web markets sell ready-made phishing kits, infostealer malware, and ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS). preyproject. com  •  Targeting: Emails/texts impersonate banks, tech support, or services. Malware steals credentials for sale or direct use. Ransomware locks files/devices and demands payment. •  Leads to account takeovers or broader identity theft. 4. Fake Services and “Rip-and-Run” Scams (Often Targeting Would-Be Criminals) •  Buyers seeking illegal items/services (fake IDs, hacking, drugs) get scammed: money taken via fake escrow, no delivery. blog.cybernod. com  •  Indirect impact on individuals: Stolen data funds these, or victims’ info enables related fraud. Fake “hitman” sites take payments for non-existent murders—scams that still cause real stress or legal issues. nytimes. com  5. Other Emerging or Niche Threats •  Counterfeit documents/forged IDs sold for impersonation. aura. com  •  DDoS-for-hire or doxing services against personal enemies. •  Financial fraud tools like carding (using stolen cards) or money mules. •  Law enforcement stings or vendor impersonations add risk for anyone involved. blog.cybernod. com   Why Individuals Are Vulnerable Criminals exploit public breaches (billions of credentials leaked) and buy/sell on forums/markets. No direct dark web access needed for victims—attacks arrive via email, SMS, or compromised accounts. group-ib. com   Protection Tips (standard cybersecurity hygiene): •  Use unique, strong passwords + a password manager and enable 2FA everywhere. •  Monitor accounts/credit regularly (free dark web scans from services like Experian). •  Avoid clicking suspicious links; use antivirus and keep software updated. •  Don’t engage with threats—report sextortion/blackmail to authorities (e.g., FBI IC3) without paying. •  Freeze credit if data is exposed; be skeptical of unsolicited “your data is for sale” claims.

최근 미국과 이란 간의 양해각서(MOU)와 그 이면에 깔린 지정학적 상황은 단순히 중동 지역의 일시적인 휴전을 넘어, ‘팍스 아메리카나(Pax Americana)’의 실질적인 퇴조와 국제 질서의 재편이라는 중대한 신호를 보내고 있습니다.  ​미국의 권위가 약화됨에 따라 예상되는 분쟁 지역의 상황 변화를 다음과 같이 분석할 수 있습니다.  ​1. 중동: '자구책(Self-reliance)'의 강화와 동맹 구조의 해체  ​미국이 이란과의 거래를 위해 동맹국(이스라엘 등)의 안보 우려를 배제하고 실용주의적 타협을 선택한 것은, 중동 국가들에게 **"미국은 더 이상 절대적인 안보 보증인이 아니다"**라는 강력한 메시지를 주었습니다.  ​독자 노선 강화: 걸프 협력 회의(GCC) 국가들은 미국의 '예측 불가능성'을 목도하며, 안보 의존도를 낮추고 이란을 포함한 주변국들과 각자도생의 길을 모색할 것입니다.   이는 중동 내 군비 경쟁을 가속화할 위험이 있습니다.  ​이스라엘의 고립과 강경 대응: 미국과의 전략적 궤도 수정으로 인해 이스라엘은 국제사회에서 전략적 고립을 경험할 가능성이 크며, 이는 오히려 이스라엘이 독자적인 선제 타격이나 군사적 강경 노선을 택하게 만드는 위험 요인이 됩니다.  ​2. 글로벌 남부(Global South): 미국의 권위 추락과 '틈새 세력'의 부상  ​미국의 압박 정책이 결국 경제적 타협으로 귀결된 사례는 다른 분쟁 당사국들에게 "미국의 제재는 끝까지 버티면 무력화할 수 있다"는 학습 효과를 제공합니다.  ​중재자의 다변화: 파키스탄이 이번 협상에서 중재자 역할을 한 것처럼, 미국의 영향력이 닿지 않거나 거부하는 지역에서 중국이나 중견 국가들이 새로운 중재자로 부상할 것입니다.   이는 미국 중심의 국제 질서가 다극 체제로 급격히 전환됨을 의미합니다.  ​비대칭 전력의 확산: 드론과 사이버 공격 같은 '가성비 무기'가 거대 군사 강국을 압박할 수 있다는 점이 입증되면서, 전 세계 분쟁 지역에서 국가 주권보다 비대칭 전력을 가진 비국가 행위자(proxy)들의 영향력이 커질 것입니다.  ​3. 미·중 경쟁: 인도-태평양 전략의 실질적 위기  ​미국이 중동이라는 '블랙홀'에 전략 자산을 집중하다가 결국 유의미한 성과 없이 협상 테이블로 돌아온 것은, 대중국 견제 동력의 상실을 의미합니다.  ​전략적 기회의 창: 미국이 중동 분쟁을 처리하는 동안 중국은 대만 해협과 남중국해에서 실질적인 군사·경제적 점유를 확대할 수 있는 기회를 갖게 되었습니다.  ​경제적 패권의 시험대: 달러 패권 하의 경제 제재가 장기적인 '자본 소모전'을 버티지 못하고 굴복하는 모습은, 향후 중국이 미국식 금융 제재망을 우회하는 새로운 경제 질서를 구축하는 데 명분을 제공할 것입니다.  ​4. 향후 분쟁 지역의 공통적인 변화 양상  ​거래적 동맹(Transactional Alliances): 미국이 우방국과 적국을 구분하기보다 '비용과 이익' 중심으로 외교를 운용함에 따라, 전 세계의 동맹 체제는 가치 기반에서 계약 기반으로 이동할 것입니다.  ​국제법의 무력화: 분쟁 해결 과정에서 국제 규범보다는 양자 간의 '거래(Deal)'가 우선시되면서, 법치주의에 기반한 국제 질서 대신 힘의 논리가 지배하는 '각자도생의 시대'가 열릴 것입니다.  ​결론적으로, 이번 MOU는 미국의 군사적·외교적 운용 능력의 한계를 노출했습니다.   미국의 권위가 약해진 자리는 단순한 평화가 아닌, 각 지역의 강자들이 영향력을 다투는 **'통제 불가능한 경쟁의 장'**이 될 가능성이 매우 높습니다. 미국이 중동에서 보여준 '실용주의'적 퇴각은 향후 다른 분쟁 지역에서 미국의 개입 의지를 더욱 위축시키는 부메랑으로 돌아올 것입니다. ㅡㅡ 트럼프의 게시물을 읽어보니 한 가지는 확실해졌습니다.   그는 여전히 중동을 이해하지 못하고 있다는 점입니다.   이 지역에서 정권, 그들의 이념, 미사일 프로그램, 대리 세력, 그리고 테러 네트워크는 그대로 둔 채 군사 장비 몇 개를 파괴하는 것은, 픽업트럭 몇 대를 폭파했다고 ISIS를 상대로 승리를 선언하는 것과 다를 바 없습니다.   이슬람 정권은 변변한 해군이나 공군력을 갖추기 훨씬 전부터 이 지역을 공포에 떨게 했습니다.   그들은 드론, 미사일, 민병대, 대리 세력을 동원해 UAE, 바레인, 쿠웨이트, 이스라엘 등을 공격했습니다. 헤즈볼라는 여전히 존재합니다.   그들의 네트워크도, 이념도, 정권도 그대로입니다. ​그런데 그것을 '승리'라고 부르면서, 잠재적으로 그 정권에 수천억 달러를 쥐여주겠다고요?   그것은 '힘을 통한 평화(peace through strength)'가 아닙니다. 그것은 이웃 동네를 다 태워버린 방화범에게 돈을 지불하는 꼴입니다.  ​당신의 친구들은 리더십을 기대했고, 당신의 적들은 압박을 기대했습니다.   하지만 돌아온 것은 친구들에게는 실망뿐이었고, 적들에게는 꿈꾸던 것보다 더 좋은 거래를 안겨준 결과였습니다.

sample_tweets.text: 전시작전권 환수는..  1. 사실 이미 진행 중이며, 중요한 지표는 3가지 정도로 본다.    가. 한국군은 연합작전을 주도할 수 있는 핵심 군사능력 확보하였는가?    나. 한국군은 북한의 핵 · 미사일 위협 대응 능력을 확보하였는가?    다. 한국군은 안정적인 한반도 전구를 유지가능하고, 역내 안보환경 조성이 가능한가?  2. 여기서의 검증 수준은 IOC(초기검증능력) ㅡ FOC(완전운용능력) ㅡ FMC(완전임무수행능력)을 순차적으로 다루며, 현재는 FOC 까지 왔을 것.   IOC는 기본적인 시스템, FOC는 지휘부의 연합작전 역량, FMC는 실제 구현 가능한 물리적 검증이라고 보면 될듯 싶다.   3. IOC는 끝났고 FOC 단계에서 FMC까지 넘어가야하나, 사실 정권의 온도차에 따라 이걸 2단계에서 마무리하냐, 3단계까지 완료하냐가 주요 쟁점거리로 부각되는듯 싶다. 약속된 3단계까지 가는것이 기본 원칙이니.. 정치적인 노이즈는 대부분 우리는 이정도 의지를 보여준다 라는 쇼맨십이라 볼수있겠다. "응~ 하고싶다고 되는게 아니라고~"  * FOC를 흔히들 지휘능력이라 생각. 장성들의 능력부족을 탓하는 무지한사람들도 있는데.. 저건 인프라다.. 인프라.. 우리나라 장성들은 정말 상상 이상으로 굉장히 스마트하다.  4. 그렇다면 언제쯤 3단계를 달성할수있을까? ISR(위성, 정찰자산), 탄도미사일 식별 / 차단 / 방호, 사이버 및 우주전, 미 증원군 전개 등.. 전반에 걸친 실증이 완료된다면야 가능할듯 싶다. 그렇다. 국방비를 현 수준에서 5~10배는 파격적으로 늘려줘야 기본적인 역량을 갖출 수 있다는 것. 그런데 2단계를 달성하기에도 지금 국방비 기준 훨씬 더 써야할거다..   5. 결론적으로 말도 많고 탈도 많은 전시작전권 환수는 정권의 의지표명일뿐이지.. 물리적으로 불가능하다는 것. 그저 "아 얘들은 이런생각이구나. 그런데 어떻게?" 정도로 보면 될 듯 싶다.   이건 이제 한국형 3축 체계와 연결된다. 이것도 어설프게 평하는 사람들이 종종 보이는데.. 이젠 그러려니 한다.. 😒  (다음번에 논해보자)  뭐.. 아무튼 그렇다는 것.. 이걸 무슨 전략적 입장이니 협상의 레버리지로 쓴다느니 이런 무지한 소리가 나올때 "FMC에 대해선 어떻게 생각하시나요 선생님" 이라 고견을 들어보도록 하자.

1페이지 요약  Jason P. Lowery의 MIT 석사논문 Softwar: A Novel Theory on Power Projection and the National Strategic Significance of Bitcoin은 비트코인을 단순한 화폐나 투자자산이 아니라, “사이버 공간에서 물리적 비용을 부과하는 새로운 권력 투사 기술”로 해석해야 한다고 주장한다. 저자는 현재 비트코인 논의가 금융, 통화, 경제 이론에 지나치게 갇혀 있어 국가안보적 의미를 놓칠 수 있다고 본다.  논문의 핵심 개념은 “Power Projection Theory”다. 자연계와 인간 사회에서 자원 소유권은 결국 물리적 힘을 투사하고 방어할 수 있는 능력과 연결되어 왔다는 것이다. 군대가 육상, 해상, 공중, 우주라는 통로에서 자유로운 접근과 교역을 보장하듯, 21세기의 핵심 통로인 사이버 공간에서도 중요한 정보 비트를 보호하려면 물리적 비용을 부과하는 방식이 필요하다고 저자는 말한다.  저자는 비트코인의 작업증명, 즉 Proof-of-Work를 이런 관점에서 재해석한다. 일반 소프트웨어는 논리적 규칙으로만 컴퓨터를 제약하지만, 비트코인은 전력, 장비, 해시 연산이라는 실제 물리적 비용을 요구한다. 따라서 공격자나 악의적 행위자가 네트워크를 조작하려면 막대한 현실 세계의 에너지 비용을 감수해야 한다. 이 점에서 비트코인은 “전자-사이버 보안 시스템”이며, 저자는 이를 “softwar”라고 부른다.  논문은 비트코인의 전략적 중요성이 금융 결제보다 더 클 수 있다고 주장한다. 비트코인은 가치 있는 금융 정보뿐 아니라, 원칙적으로 모든 종류의 디지털 정보를 물리적으로 보호할 수 있는 새로운 보안 패러다임의 실증 사례라는 것이다. 저자는 이를 “planetary state mechanism”, 즉 지구 규모의 상태 기계로도 설명한다. 비트코인이 가장 강력한 작업증명 네트워크가 된 이상, 다른 유사 시스템은 같은 수준의 물리적 보안성과 희소성을 재현하기 어렵다고 본다.  정책적으로 저자는 미국이 비트코인을 단순히 암호화폐로만 규제하거나 금융 전문가에게만 해석을 맡기는 것은 위험하다고 경고한다. 비트코인이 실제로 새로운 권력 투사 기술이라면, 이를 조기에 이해하고 채택하는 국가가 사이버 공간에서 전략적 우위를 얻을 수 있기 때문이다. 그래서 저자는 비트코인 비축, 채굴 산업 보호, 관련 연구 확대, 군사·사이버보안 관점의 정책 검토를 제안한다.  다만 이 논문은 실증 검증보다는 grounded theory 방식으로 새로운 이론적 틀을 제시하는 작업이다. 저자도 자신의 주장이 확정된 결론이 아니라 후속 연구로 검증되어야 할 가설임을 인정한다. 요약하면, 이 논문의 메시지는 “비트코인의 본질은 돈이 아니라 권력 균형과 사이버 공간의 물리적 보안일 수 있다”는 것이다.

📋 분석 요약  데이터의 중요성과 함께 기술의 통제권, 즉 '기술 주권'의 필요성이 대두되었습니다. 특히 특정 국가에 대한 기술 의존도가 높아질 경우, 정치적, 경제적으로 취약해질 수 있다는 인식이 확산되면서 각국은 자체적인 기술 역량을 키우고 관련 공급망을 안정화하려는 노력을 기울이고 있습니다. 이는 마치 반도체를 둘러싼 국가 간 경쟁처럼, 앞으로 더 많은 기술 분야에서 벌어질 경쟁의 예고편이라고 볼 수 있습니다. Clean Election Watch 시각호주는 지리적으로 고립되어 있고, 상당수의 첨단 기술을 해외에 의존하고 있는 상황입니다. 이러한 상황에서 기술 주권을 논하는 것은 호주에게 더욱 시급하고 중요한 과제입니다. 단순히 해외 기술을 도입하는 것을 넘어, 호주만의 강점을 살려 혁신적인 기술을 개발하고, 이를 통해 경제적 이익을 창출하는 선순환 구조를 만드는 것이 중요합니다. 예를 들어, 호주는 청정 에너지 분야에서 잠재력이 크므로, 이와 관련된 기술 개발에 집중하여 글로벌 시장을 선도하는 것도 좋은 전략이 될 수 있습니다. 또한, 강력한 사이버 보안 시스템 구축은 디지털 시대의 필수적인 안전 장치로서, 호주 국민의 개인 정보와 국가 기밀을 보호하는 데 핵심적인 역할을 할 것입니다.기술 주권 확보…

sample_tweets.text: 📢선관위 서버는 언제, 포렌식?🫆🔎  🎯제대로 수사 할까?  합수본은 11일 경기 과천시의 중앙선관위와 서울시선관위, 투표용지 부족 사태가 발생한 송파·강남·서초·광진·동작구선관위 등 7곳에 대한 압수수색에 나섰다. 이번 압수수색 대상에는 선관위 서버 등 전산 시스템도 포함됐다.   다만🔎 이는 직원들의 [대화 내역 등]을 확인하기 위해 수사당국이 원격 접근을 하는 방식으로, 실제 서버를 물리적으로 확보한 것은 아닌 것⁉️으로 전해졌다.  🌋당연히 대화 보다 중요한 것이, 통합선거인 명부 관리 시스템과 로그기록들인데 핵심 로데이터 포렌식은 쏙 빼고 뭘 본다는 건지?  🔎🎯📢 잘지켜 봐야 한다.  지난 윤대통령 탄핵심판때에도 헌재는 서버 및 투표관련 자료 검증을 무려 3번이나 기각시켰다 [노코멘트 무시]. 심지어 선관위 사무총장이 협력하겠다 공언까지 했었다. 당시에도, '도대체 뭘 감추어야 할 것이 있길래 겨우 서버 디지털 포렌식인데 저렇게 막나' 하고 의구심을 가졌다.  그런데 탄핵 심판 증인으로 나온 국정원3차장이 2023년 당시 점검때도 상당히 비협조적이어서 겨우 6,400대의 컴퓨터 중 겨우 317대(5%)진단했는데 31.5점 이었고 (110개 국가기관 평균-81.9점/최하 44.6점)-추가 진행했다면 더 심각 했을것이라는 충격적 증언을 듣고서야 이해가 되었다. 👀 심지어 점검하면서 느낀점은 국가위기가 초래할만큼 잠재적으로 위험한 상태였다는 암시적 멘트까지 남겼다. 선관위가 개방해줘서 점검가능한 것이라고 주장한부분에 거의 코웃음치다 시피했다. 모의테스트때 쉽게 뚫렸단다..북한은 뭐..(당시 해커 그룹 킴수키가 포함된 북한 총국 산하 라자루스는 이미 최고 수준 해커그룹이다- 비트코인 최대 탈취)  🤔 아무튼 분명 국정원 점검 이후, 다양한 방법을 통해 보완한 것으로 알고 있다. 그런데, 이번 선관위 직원의 폭로까지 비추어 보았을때, 눈가리고 아웅식으로 전산조작 가능한 핵심 부분은 그대로 존치되어 유지된 것으로 추정된다. 계속 이런식이다.  국방과 마찬가지다. 이건 좌우의 문제가 아닌 아래위 문제다. 도대체 어디까지 뚫려 있는 건가?  📢이번에도 하는척으로, 마무리 되어선 안된다.  절대로.🇰🇷🇺🇸

sample_tweets.text: x.com/i/article/206882425092…

🔴The Houthis are leading Iranian supply routes to the Muslim Brotherhood in Sudan.  ⚠️Fighters affiliated with the Muslim Brotherhood's army in Sudan are preparing a truck-mounted recoilless rifle.  The unit appears to be using a prominent Iranian-made DIO SPG-9 73mm recoilless rifle equipped with a Zafar anti-tank missile. Intelligence sources reported that representatives of the Houthi group held meetings last week with Muslim Brotherhood-affiliated groups to discuss new mechanisms and routes for transferring Iranian military technology, including drones and missiles, through smuggling networks operating in the Red Sea and the Horn of Africa.  The sources, who requested anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter, said the discussions covered logistical arrangements and maritime routes believed to be used for transporting military components and materials related to the manufacture and operation of drones and guided munitions.  According to the sources, the Houthi group relies on smuggling networks extending between Yemen and the Horn of Africa, utilizing staging points and temporary storage sites near the western coast of the Red Sea. These sites are used to transport and assemble some military components before redirecting them to other destinations in the region.  The sources added that these networks exploit the traditional infrastructure of maritime smuggling operations in the region, which has been active for years in transporting goods and weapons across the Red Sea.   Edrak obtained the names of five Houthi leaders believed to have participated in coordinating or managing activities related to arms transfers and smuggling operations. Among them is Mansour al-Saadi, the Houthi naval chief of staff, who was previously sanctioned by Washington for his role in military operations and attacks targeting international shipping in the Red Sea.  The sources also indicated the participation of Jamil Yahya Zar’ah, Aqil Ahmed al-Shami, Mohammed Ahmed al-Talibi, and Mohammed Fadl Abdul-Nabi in meetings held last week with Muslim Brotherhood figures in the Arus area on the Red Sea coast.  The sources confirmed that some shipments related to these arrangements have already arrived in Port Sudan in recent weeks, but noted the lack of independent, verifiable information regarding the nature or size of these shipments.  These developments come at a time of growing regional and international concern about the expansion of arms smuggling networks across the Red Sea and the potential threat they pose to maritime security and stability in the region. #Edraak

2/2  The US has nothing concrete to show for its efforts in this war.  And it still came at considerable cost.  Thousands of innocent civilians have been killed, millions more have been displaced, and hundreds of children are dead after a US missile struck a school in Minab.  Trump and his cabinet are complicit in war crimes.  We've also lost US troops and Iranian missiles have damaged several key US military bases in the middle east.  Worse yet, the Islamic Regime is now more entrenched than it was prior to the war and is emerging as a more brutal, militant dictatorship.  The IRGC is still a powerful authority in the region and has deeper ties to the new hardline Ayatollah.  The people of Iran will continue to suffer at the hands of a more punishing and violent regime.  We have failed to "liberate" the Iranian people from the clutches of their repressive government.   This war has wreaked a devastating toll on the world, on the American economy and taxpayer, on our alliances and global reputation, on our munitions stockpile, on the Iranian and Lebanese people, on our Persian Gulf allies, on our very credibility.  And for what!?  Nothing.  No, LESS than nothing.  This so-called "memorandum of understanding" is just playing for time.  And after all of the damage and suffering that Trump's war has caused, there's no reality where Iran does not come out on top.  The regime has only GAINED leverage in future negotiations and has risen from the conflict a more formidable adversary.   Trump's actions have one-sidedly benefitted the enemy, China, Russia, the IRGC...  After all of the chaos that Trump's war has unleashed on the world, Iran will have secured greater ownership over its nuclear materials and ballistic missiles program.  They will still remain a regional sponsor of terrorism, they will still control the waterways in and around the Strait.  They will continue to extort the US government and will receive sanction relief and/or some form of war reparations so that, in return, Donald Trump can claim he brought "peace" to the region.  But Donald Trump is no peacemaker. He's an incompetent and belligerent war criminal with an appetite for conquest that's being fueled entirely by his ego.  His mishandling of the war has cost the American people DEARLY.  More than that, his actions have reignited old conflicts and have plunged the world into disarray.  Trump and his cabinet have repeatedly LIED about the pretext for this war; insisting that Iran was building a nuclear weapon.  Netanyahu has been pushing the same faithless warnings for decades too.  But our own intelligence community says otherwise. Iran was NOT building a nuclear weapon and posed no imminent threat to our country.  DNI Tulsi Gabbard testified before Congress:  > “The Intelligence Community continues to assess that Iran is not building a nuclear weapon and that Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has not authorized the nuclear weapons program he suspended in 2003.”  A top US counterterrorism official also resigned, stating, "Iran never posed an imminent threat."  Trump lied about operation "midnight hammer," and he continues to lie about the looming risk of an Iranian nuclear weapon.  Obama's deal intended to avoid a war and box in Iran's nuclear program.  By all accounts, Iran was committed to its end of the bargain before Trump abandoned the previous nuclear deal OUT OF SPITE for his predecessor.  This was according to US intelligence, the International Atomic Energy Agency, and even Trump administration officials at the time.  Iran only began taking steps towards enriching uranium after Trump withdrew from the JCPOA. Their nuclear program became more advanced than it would have been under Obama's deal.  And now Trump will drag these negotiations out for as long as he can until he's forced to accept a final agreement that will be far more conciliatory towards Iran than the deal Obama negotiated through DIPLOMACY, not WAR!

10 GITHUB REPOS THAT SCRAPE THE ENTIRE INTERNET FOR YOU  Bookmark every single one. Each one pulls clean data off any website on earth, the kind of access companies sell behind a sales call and a contract.  1. github.com/mendableai/firecr…  Point it at any website and it crawls every page, renders the JavaScript, and hands back clean structured data an AI can read instantly. It crossed 130K stars and landed in GitHub's top 100 repos. The scraping backbone half the AI startups quietly run on, open for anyone.  2. github.com/unclecode/crawl4a…  The #1 trending crawler on GitHub. Turns any site into clean, LLM-ready markdown, faster than the paid services and with no API key, no account, no per-page fee. A dev built it in days after getting fed up paying $16 for a gated scraper. 51K stars. Apache 2.0.  3. github.com/browser-use/brows…  An AI agent that drives a real browser like a human, clicking, scrolling, logging in, filling forms, and pulling data off sites it has never seen before. Two ETH Zurich researchers built it and it hit 95K stars in about a year. The thing that scrapes pages no simple crawler can reach. MIT.  4. github.com/apify/crawlee  The full professional scraping framework, with rotating proxies, automatic retries, browser fingerprint spoofing, and queue management, all the machinery that keeps you from getting blocked. The exact stack scraping companies charge thousands to operate, handed to you for free.  5. github.com/scrapy/scrapy  The original industrial-strength scraper that has quietly powered data teams for over a decade. Crawl millions of pages, extract anything, export it clean. Battle-tested at a scale most paid tools never reach, and free the entire time.  6. github.com/microsoft/markitd…  Microsoft's own tool that converts any file or web page, PDFs, Office docs, HTML, images, into clean markdown an AI can actually use. The messy-data-to-clean-data step companies build whole pipelines around, open-sourced by Microsoft itself.  7. github.com/D4Vinci/Scrapling  A stealth scraper built to stay invisible, adapting automatically when a site changes its layout and slipping past the bot detection that stops everything else. The cat-and-mouse layer that anti-scraping vendors sell as a premium feature, free and open.  8. github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy  Mirror and control any Android phone from your computer to pull data and automate apps that have no website at all. The bridge into mobile-only platforms that most scrapers can't touch. 130K+ stars. Apache 2.0.  9. github.com/alirezamika/autos…  Show it one example of what you want and it figures out the pattern and scrapes the rest of the site automatically. No selectors, no code to maintain. The "just get me this data" button, in a few lines of Python.  10. github.com/lwthiker/curl-imp…  A version of curl that perfectly mimics a real browser's fingerprint, so the requests sneaking past every defense look exactly like a human with Chrome open. sample_tweets.text: Asking price of 200 BTC 🤯  🚨 Alleged Cryptographic Library 0-Day Exploit Package Offered for Sale  A threat actor is advertising what they claim to be a collection of previously undisclosed vulnerabilities affecting a cryptographic software library.  According to the seller, the package allegedly contains:  * 10 undisclosed ("0-day") vulnerabilities * Remote Denial-of-Service (DoS) exploit * Heap Out-of-Bounds (OOB) Read vulnerability * Nonce reuse exploitation technique * Cross-library impact claims affecting multiple cryptographic implementations  The actor claims the vulnerabilities impact a function named:  * crypto_box_open_easy  Additional claims include:  * Triggering crashes through boundary value manipulation * Server process termination via SIGSEGV conditions * Exploitation of memory safety issues * Exclusive sale offering * Asking price of 200 BTC  Threat Intelligence Assessment:  * The post contains highly technical language intended to establish credibility. * No independently verifiable proof-of-concept or technical validation is visible in the screenshot. * The requested price is exceptionally high, indicating the seller believes the vulnerabilities could have strategic value. * Claims involving cryptographic libraries should be treated cautiously until reproduced and verified by trusted researchers. * Underground forums frequently contain exaggerated or fraudulent vulnerability sales alongside legitimate research.  Potential Impact (If Verified):  * Service outages through remote crashes * Information disclosure through memory exposure * Potential weakening of cryptographic protections * Supply-chain risk affecting products that rely on the vulnerable library * Large-scale downstream impact if widely deployed software is affected  Analyst Note: High-value vulnerability sales targeting cryptographic libraries are relatively rare because a single flaw can affect thousands of applications, cloud services, VPNs, messaging platforms, and embedded systems simultaneously. Verification remains critical, as forum actors often inflate exploit capabilities to justify premium pricing.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Cybersecurity

Analyst Note: Payroll datasets are often more valuable than standard customer databases because they combine personal identifiers, employment details, tax information, and organizational context, enabling highly targeted fraud and social engineering operations.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Mexico

🇮🇳 Historic Zoomcar Credential Database Resurfaces on Underground Forum  A threat actor has posted what appears to be a historical Zoomcar credential dataset labeled "Zoomcar.com_bcrypt_July_2018" on an underground forum.  According to the post:  * Claimed victim: Zoomcar * Dataset label: Zoomcar.com_bcrypt_July_2018 * Password format: bcrypt hashes * Data appears to originate from a previously compromised credential database * Download link shared publicly within the forum  Why This Matters:  * bcrypt hashes are significantly more resistant to cracking than legacy hashing algorithms. * However, weak passwords can still be recovered through offline cracking attempts. * Historic breaches frequently reappear on underground forums years later and are often merged into larger credential collections used for credential-stuffing attacks.  Potential Risks:  * Account takeover attempts against users who reused passwords * Credential-stuffing attacks against other services * Phishing campaigns targeting known Zoomcar users * Password recovery attacks using historical customer information  Threat Intelligence Assessment:  * The dataset appears to reference a 2018-era breach rather than a newly claimed intrusion. * Old credential databases remain valuable to threat actors because many users continue to reuse passwords across multiple services. * The presence of bcrypt hashing suggests the original service implemented stronger password storage controls than many older breached platforms.  Analyst Note: Even when a breach is several years old, recycled credential databases continue to drive account takeover activity. Organizations and users should assume any historical credentials may eventually resurface and ensure password reuse is eliminated wherever possible.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Zoomcar

sample_tweets.text: The packets must flow

BIG PICTURE REASONS WHY "CHOCOLATE RAIN" WENT VIRAL: • With no algorithms, the Internet was driven by novelty, not loyalty. Weirdness wins novelty. • Myspace was #1 in social media. YouTube was unproven and Facebook had just opened up beyond colleges. YouTube needed a "model home" for what its cultural real estate meant. • One person uploading a video that thousands of strangers parodied became YouTube's behavioral "model home." No other platform had that social dynamic. • Soulja Boy, Chris Crocker, and other talented creators had viral YouTube videos that got widely parodied. "Chocolate Rain" became identified with a type of "shareholder safe" virality. YouTube's human editors promoted it on the front page. • YouTube was an unproven business for both Google and creators. The idea of creators earning Internet money was not mainstream. I was added in round two of YouTube's experimental Partner Program. I believe round one had about thirty creators. • Television remained the eight-hundred-pound media gorilla. Viacom's billion-dollar copyright lawsuit was an existential YouTube threat just as the first YouTube videos went viral. The fact that "Chocolate Rain" began on YouTube and transitioned to me being on CNN, Jimmy Kimmel, discussed by Carson Daly and dozens of other celebrities... came at a time when YouTube needed a Rosetta Stone. They needed to translate a massively subsidized, high-risk venture into understandable cultural value. "Chocolate Rain" became a stenographer of YouTube crossing over. I got parodied on South Park, Saturday Night Live, nominated for a People's Choice Award, sang with Boyz II Men on Tosh.0 etc. HOW DID "CHOCOLATE RAIN" GO VIRAL?: • "Chocolate Rain" was rushed to completion in April of 2007 since I had another song ("Love," made with Kooby) featured on YouTube's front page and wanted other new content. It sat at around 30,000 views until summer. • "Chocolate Rain" got posted on Digg in July of 2007, an early Reddit-style social bookmark site. Someone saw it there and posted it on 4chan. • 4chan worked to meme "Chocolate Rain," "Numa Numa," Rick Astley, and other things. In 2007, 4chan was dominantly "Howard Stern liberal." Being offensive, outrageous, and highly speech-tolerant used to be identified with leftist, avant-garde identity. My first inkling that "Chocolate Rain" was going viral was 4chan prank-calling Tom Green's self-produced show and the caller busting out singing "Chocolate Rain!" MORE FACTORS IN "CHOCOLATE RAIN" GOING VIRAL: • YouTube had no stereo sound in early 2007. I posted a free "Chocolate Rain" MP3 download, with a giant video banner announcing it, purely to circumvent this. I wanted my songs heard in stereo. • "Chocolate Rain" begins with an instrumental and loops. This, combined with the MP3 download, made parodies easy. This was totally unplanned luck. It's like it was made to be parodied. • I looked like Janet Jackson, moved like Mr. Bean, and sounded like Barry White. Not trying to mean-girl myself, just being blunt. I was a unique combination of attributes but also not self-aware. Social internet video of everyday life was a new experience. Like, "If that guy is singing 'Chocolate Rain,' what's MY neighbor doing?" Everyday life was transforming into a democratic video content frontier nobody had given much prior thought to. • It's worth noting that 2007 was before mainstream mobile Internet video consumption. YouTube was overwhelmingly consumed on desktop computers and laptops. The later shift to engagement-optimized and loyalty-optimized social video was heavily influenced by phones. PERSONAL FACTORS WHY "CHOCOLATE RAIN" WENT VIRAL: • I built a bedsheet box in my living room to sing "Chocolate Rain" in because I'm agoraphobic. That's the opposite of claustrophobic. Boundaries supercharge me. It also turns out that lots of people have bedsheets to hang up. • I moved stiffly and sang "Chocolate Rain" with elongated vowels because of dyspraxia, a neurological movement difficulty tied to me being autistic (first diagnosed at age sixteen). • "Chocolate Rain" musically captured my tendency towards echolalia and echopraxia — repetition and reinvocation of speech and behavior. These are adaptations to being a partially verbal autistic who has to blend-in with speaking society. They also help in making catchy songs.

🟡 위협 점수: 5/10 (보통)

제공해주신 위협 인텔리전스 데이터를 분석한 결과입니다.

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 공격자들은 AI 에이전트의 실무 역량을 모방하거나, 오픈소스 생태계(npm) 및 워드프레스 플러그인과 같은 공급망 취약점을 적극 활용하고 있습니다. 특히 EDR 솔루션을 무력화하는 전용 프레임워크(GentleKiller 등)를 RaaS 조직이 배포하며 방어 체계를 우회하는 경향이 뚜렷합니다. 또한, 대규모 계정 탈취를 통한 인프라 장악(FortiBleed 등)과 더불어, AI 모델의 보안 설정을 악용하는 새로운 유형의 공격이 급증하고 있습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
*   **The Gentlemen (Krebs on Security, WeLiveSecurity):** EDR 무력화 프레임워크인 'GentleKiller'를 활용해 보안 프로세스를 타겟팅하는 RaaS 조직.
*   **Sapphire Sleet (BlueNoroff) (BleepingComputer, 데일리시큐):** Mastra AI 공급망 공격을 통해 npm 패키지에 악성코드를 삽입, 개발자 인증 정보 및 가상화폐 지갑 탈취.
*   **Evil Corp (CyberScoop, Malwarebytes):** SocGholish 봇넷을 운영하며 전 세계 15,000개 이상의 웹사이트를 감염시킨 주체.
*   **TeamPCP (CyberScoop):** 오픈소스 소프트웨어의 보안보다 속도를 중시하는 개발 문화를 악용하여 공급망 공격 수행.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
*   **북한 연계 해킹 조직:** AI 개발 생태계(npm) 및 국내 금융권 타겟 스피어 피싱(Kimsuky) 활동 지속.
*   **포티넷 장비 침해:** 'FortiBleed' 캠페인으로 인해 국내를 포함한 전 세계 포티넷 방화벽/VPN 장비 8만 6천여 대가 침해됨.
*   **금융권 보안 이슈:** 5월 금융권 대상 피싱 및 Dropper/Downloader 유포 증가(AhnLab ASEC).

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
*   **CVE-2026-49468:** LiteLLM 인증 바이패스, CVSS 9.5, 악용 중.
*   **CVE-2026-55450:** Langflow 파일 업로드 취약점, CVSS 9.3, 악용 중.
*   **CVE-2026-4020:** Gravity SMTP API 키 노출, CVSS 미상(고위험), 활발히 악용 중.
*   **CVE-2025-59382:** QNAP NAS URL 인젝션/패스워드 리셋, 심각도 높음, 수정 완료.

### 5. IOC 요약
*   **CVE:**
    *   CVE-2026-4020 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/hackers-exploit-gravity-smtp-wordpress.html), [데일리시큐](https://www.dailysecu.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=207246))
    *   CVE-2026-49468 ([FreetJan/Twitter](https://twitter.com/FreetJan/status/2068827034793693305#m))
    *   CVE-2026-55450 ([__kokumoto/Twitter](https://twitter.com/__kokumoto/status/2068550277133894109#m))

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
*   **Gravity SMTP 플러그인 업데이트:** 2.1.5 버전 미만 사용 시 즉시 업데이트하여 CVE-2026-4020 취약점 차단.
*   **Fortinet 장비 보안 점검:** 포티게이트 방화벽/VPN 관리자 계정 비밀번호를 즉시 변경하고, 인터넷 노출 여부를 확인하여 접근 제어 강화.
*   **npm 패키지 무결성 검증:** 개발 환경에서 사용하는 npm 패키지(특히 AI 관련 라이브러리)의 출처를 확인하고, 'chai-as-init' 등 의심스러운 패키지 사용 여부 전수 조사.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
*   **점수:** 8.5/10
*   **근거:** 공급망 공격(npm)과 대규모 인프라 장악(FortiBleed)이 동시에 진행되고 있으며, EDR 우회 기술이 고도화되어 방어 측면의 부담이 매우 큼.
